subtopic_detail: Hyperemesis gravidarum admission and refeeding risk
stem:
A 24-year-old gravida 1 para 0 woman presents to your emergency department at 9 weeks gestation with severe vomiting for 10 days. She tells you 'I can't keep water down for more than a few minutes'. She has lost 5 kg from her pre-pregnancy weight of 64 kg. She has had ginger, B6, oral metoclopramide, and ondansetron prescribed by her general practitioner without lasting effect. She has no abdominal pain, no fever, and no urinary symptoms.

On examination she has dry mucous membranes and a soft abdomen. Urinalysis shows ketones 3+ with no nitrites or leukocytes. Bloods show sodium 132 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 2.9 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), chloride 90 mmol/L, bicarbonate 30 mmol/L, urea 8 mmol/L, creatinine 84 micromol/L. Beta-hCG is appropriate for gestation; thyroid function is mildly suppressed (TSH 0.3 mIU/L). She has not eaten reliably for nearly two weeks.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 112, BP 100/60 mmHg standing 92/56 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, capillary refill 3 seconds, abdomen soft non-tender, fundus not palpable
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis ketones 3+; sodium 132 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 2.9 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), bicarbonate 30 mmol/L, urea 8 mmol/L, creatinine 84 micromol/L; beta-hCG appropriate for gestation; TSH 0.3 mIU/L; transabdominal ultrasound single live intrauterine pregnancy matched for dates
data_table.Medications: Ondansetron 8 mg orally (last dose 6 hours ago), metoclopramide PRN, ginger, pyridoxine 25 mg three times daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit for intravenous thiamine, intravenous fluid with potassium replacement, then escalate antiemetics
options.rationale: Severe hyperemesis with ketonuria, hypokalaemia, and orthostatic hypotension warrants admission. Intravenous thiamine 100 mg is given BEFORE any dextrose-containing fluid (Wernicke prevention), followed by isotonic crystalloid with potassium replacement and then escalation to combination antiemetic therapy (e.g. ondansetron with cyclizine, or short-course oral prednisolone in refractory cases). (Source: RANZCOG; AMH)

options.text: Commence intravenous 5% dextrose 1 L over 1 hour with intramuscular cyclizine for symptom control
options.rationale: Dextrose-containing fluid before thiamine in a malnourished hyperemesis patient risks precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy; thiamine is always given first. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Discharge with oral doxylamine-pyridoxine combination tablet and outpatient antenatal follow-up next week
options.rationale: Doxylamine-pyridoxine is appropriate first-line outpatient therapy for mild nausea, but this patient has dehydration, electrolyte derangement, and treatment failure on standard antiemetics; she needs admission. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Arrange immediate termination of pregnancy referral given the severity of the maternal vomiting and weight loss
options.rationale: Termination is reserved for life-threatening maternal compromise that does not respond to maximal therapy; standard escalation including IV fluids and steroids has not yet been tried. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Commence high-dose oral prednisolone 60 mg daily before any intravenous fluid or thiamine is given to her
options.rationale: Oral steroids are a salvage therapy for refractory hyperemesis after standard antiemetics and intravenous rehydration fail; they are not first-line and the patient cannot reliably absorb oral medication when actively vomiting. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum is severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy producing weight loss, dehydration, electrolyte abnormality, and ketonuria. Admission criteria include failure of outpatient management, dehydration, electrolyte derangement, or significant weight loss. Thiamine 100 mg must precede any dextrose-containing fluid because malnourished women are at risk of Wernicke encephalopathy. Stepwise antiemetics include ondansetron, metoclopramide, cyclizine, and short-course corticosteroids for refractory cases.
key_points: Thiamine before dextrose - Wernicke prevention is non-negotiable
Admit for IV fluids if ketonuria, electrolyte derangement, or treatment-failed outpatient
Stepwise antiemetics: doxylamine-pyridoxine, metoclopramide, ondansetron, cyclizine, steroids
Mildly suppressed TSH from beta-hCG cross-reactivity is benign in early pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Intravenous thiamine 100 mg in 100 mL saline given over 30 minutes precedes any dextrose-containing fluid in malnourished hyperemesis admissions to prevent acute Wernicke encephalopathy.

stem:
A 29-year-old primigravida attends your antenatal clinic at 14 weeks gestation for routine review. She is well, with no urinary symptoms, no flank pain, and no contraction-like discomfort. Her booking-visit midstream urine culture has just returned: Escherichia coli at greater than 10^8 CFU/L, sensitive to nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim, cefalexin, and amoxicillin-clavulanate. She has no penicillin allergy and no other medical history. Booking renal function and blood pressure are normal.

On examination she is afebrile. Abdomen is soft with a fundus consistent with dates. Urinalysis at the clinic visit is now negative for nitrites or leukocyte esterase. She asks 'do I really need antibiotics if I feel fine?'.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, fundus consistent with dates, no renal angle tenderness, no costovertebral tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Booking midstream urine culture E. coli >10^8 CFU/L, sensitivities as above; today urinalysis nitrites and leukocyte esterase negative; routine antenatal bloods within normal limits
data_table.Medications: Pregnancy multivitamin including iodine and folate

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure that asymptomatic bacteriuria does not need antibiotic treatment in a healthy pregnancy and rescreen routinely
options.rationale: Untreated asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy substantially increases the risk of acute pyelonephritis and preterm birth; the AU standard is to treat after a confirmed positive culture. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; RANZCOG)

options.text: Commence oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days and repeat midstream urine culture 7 days post-treatment
options.rationale: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy is treated to reduce pyelonephritis and preterm birth. Nitrofurantoin is AU first-line in the first and second trimester (avoid at term and in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency given neonatal haemolysis risk); a follow-up culture confirms eradication. Cefalexin or amoxicillin-clavulanate are reasonable alternatives if nitrofurantoin is contraindicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; RANZCOG)

options.text: Repeat the urine culture in 2 weeks before deciding whether antibiotic treatment is justified for this woman
options.rationale: Two consecutive positive cultures from voided samples are reassuring of true bacteriuria, but a single positive midstream culture at greater than 10^8 CFU/L in pregnancy still warrants treatment in AU practice. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Prescribe a single oral dose of fosfomycin 3 g once and arrange routine clinic follow-up at 6 weeks
options.rationale: Single-dose fosfomycin is acceptable for non-pregnant women with uncomplicated cystitis; in pregnancy, AU practice favours a longer course with documented test-of-cure. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Refer to obstetric medicine for inpatient intravenous antibiotic treatment regardless of symptom status today
options.rationale: IV antibiotics are indicated for pyelonephritis or systemic illness, not asymptomatic bacteriuria; outpatient oral therapy is appropriate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy is screened universally at booking. Treatment reduces the risk of pyelonephritis (which complicates approximately 30% of untreated cases) and preterm birth. AU first-line is nitrofurantoin or cefalexin for 5-7 days, with a test-of-cure culture afterwards. Trimethoprim is avoided in the first trimester; nitrofurantoin is avoided at term and where neonatal G6PD deficiency is suspected due to haemolysis risk. Group B streptococcus bacteriuria of any colony count is treated and triggers intrapartum prophylaxis.
key_points: Universal booking screen for ASB; treat all positive cultures
Nitrofurantoin or cefalexin first-line in pregnancy; avoid trimethoprim in T1
Group B streptococcus in any concentration triggers intrapartum prophylaxis
Follow up with test-of-cure culture to confirm eradication
explanation.pearls: Nitrofurantoin 100 mg orally twice daily for 5 days is the AU first-line for asymptomatic bacteriuria in the first and second trimester; avoid at term and where the neonate is at risk of G6PD-deficiency related haemolysis.

stem:
A 24-year-old woman presents to your general practice clinic with worsening cyclical pelvic pain for 3 years, dyspareunia for 6 months, and heavy menstrual bleeding requiring tampon and pad together with overnight changes. She tells you 'the pain takes over for the first 2 days every month and I have to call in sick'. She is currently not in a relationship and does not want pregnancy in the next 2 years, but would like to preserve fertility for the future.

On bimanual examination there is uterosacral nodularity and mild tenderness; the uterus is mobile. There is no adnexal mass. Pelvic transvaginal ultrasound shows a 2 cm endometrioma on the left ovary and no other significant findings. She has no medical comorbidity, is a non-smoker, has a body mass index of 24, and her blood pressure is normal. She has tried ibuprofen and mefenamic acid which have not been adequate.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Bimanual: uterosacral nodularity and mild tenderness, mobile uterus, no adnexal mass
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound: 2 cm left ovarian endometrioma, normal uterus and right ovary; FBC haemoglobin 119 g/L (115-165), ferritin 18 micrograms/L (30-300)
data_table.Medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed; mefenamic acid 500 mg three times daily; oral iron

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer for diagnostic laparoscopy and definitive excision of all peritoneal endometriotic deposits
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is reserved for failed medical therapy, severe disease unresponsive to first-line, or large endometriomas requiring surgical management. AU practice escalates medical options first when fertility is desired and the endometrioma is small. (Source: RANZCOG; NICE NG73)

options.text: Trial GnRH agonist therapy with add-back hormone replacement for 6 months as the first hormonal option
options.rationale: GnRH analogues are second-line after first-line hormonal therapy fails because of bone density and vasomotor cost; add-back is used to mitigate these but it is not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG; AMH)

options.text: Commence continuous combined oral contraception or a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system with NSAIDs for breakthrough pain
options.rationale: AU first-line medical management of endometriosis-related pain is continuous combined oral contraception or progestogen-based therapy (oral progestogen, depot, or 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system). The 52 mg LNG-IUS additionally treats heavy menstrual bleeding and preserves fertility. NSAIDs are added for breakthrough pain. Six-month trial is expected before escalation. (Source: RANZCOG; NICE NG73)

options.text: Refer for ovarian endometrioma cystectomy now given the 2 cm finding on transvaginal ultrasound today
options.rationale: Endometriomas under 4 cm with no diagnostic uncertainty are typically managed medically when fertility is desired; surgery is reserved for larger lesions, severe symptoms, or suspicion of malignancy. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Commence danazol 200 mg orally daily for 6 months with monthly clinical and biochemical monitoring
options.rationale: Danazol is rarely used in AU practice due to androgenic adverse effects; safer hormonal options are first-line. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Endometriosis-related pain is initially managed with NSAIDs and AU first-line hormonal options (continuous combined oral contraception or progestogen-only therapy including the 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system). The LNG-IUS is particularly useful when heavy menstrual bleeding coexists and fertility preservation is desired. Failed first-line therapy escalates to GnRH analogues (with add-back) or laparoscopic excision. Small endometriomas (under 4 cm) without diagnostic uncertainty are followed medically; larger lesions or suspicion of malignancy prompt surgical referral.
key_points: First-line medical: continuous combined OCP, oral progestogen, or 52 mg LNG-IUS
GnRH analogues with add-back are second-line
Endometriomas under 4 cm: medical first; larger or suspicious: surgical
NSAIDs as adjunct, not stand-alone
explanation.pearls: The 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system reduces dysmenorrhoea, heavy menstrual bleeding, and endometriotic disease activity simultaneously, and preserves fertility - ideal first-line for women not currently planning pregnancy.

stem:
A 28-year-old woman gravida 2 para 1 presents to your tertiary delivery suite at 31 weeks plus 2 days with a confirmed gush of clear fluid 2 hours ago. She is not contracting. Her last delivery was a normal vaginal birth at term. She is well in herself. Her booking screen for group B streptococcus was negative; she has no other infection risk factors. The foetus is in cephalic presentation with reassuring antenatal records.

On sterile speculum examination, clear pooling is seen in the posterior fornix and a positive amniotic fluid test confirms ruptured membranes. The cervix appears uneffaced and closed. Maternal temperature, uterine tenderness, and foetal heart rate tracing are normal. Bedside transabdominal ultrasound shows a single foetus in cephalic position with significantly reduced amniotic fluid index but persistent foetal movements and no foetal anomalies. She has not received antenatal steroids in this pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 86, BP 116/74 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C; foetal heart rate baseline 140 bpm with reactive trace
data_table.Examination: Sterile speculum: clear pooling, no offensive odour, cervix closed; abdomen soft, non-tender, no contractions palpable
data_table.Investigations: FBC and CRP within normal limits; amniotic fluid test positive; transabdominal ultrasound single cephalic foetus, AFI reduced

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management bundle?

options.text: Immediate caesarean section regardless of cervical status given the gestation and rupture of membranes today
options.rationale: Caesarean is reserved for established labour with malpresentation, foetal compromise, chorioamnionitis with deteriorating foetus, or other obstetric indication; expectant management is the AU default at this gestation. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM)

options.text: Antenatal corticosteroids, erythromycin 10 days, magnesium sulfate if delivery within 24 hours, and expectant management with daily review
options.rationale: PPROM at 24+0 to 33+6 is managed expectantly with the AU bundle: antenatal corticosteroids (single course, 24 to 34+6 weeks), erythromycin 250 mg orally four times daily for 10 days, magnesium sulfate neuroprotection if delivery is anticipated within 24 hours, and daily clinical and CTG surveillance for chorioamnionitis or foetal compromise. Delivery is planned at 36 to 37 weeks unless infection, abruption, or non-reassuring foetal status intervenes. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM; ACOG)

options.text: Single dose corticosteroids and immediate induction of labour with synthetic oxytocin via continuous infusion
options.rationale: Induction at 31 weeks PPROM accelerates extreme preterm birth without infection drivers; expectant management improves neonatal outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM)

options.text: Administer tocolysis with nifedipine for 48 hours and discharge home with twice-weekly outpatient surveillance
options.rationale: Tocolysis in PPROM is controversial and limited to enabling steroid completion or in-utero transfer; home discharge is unsafe given infection risk. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM)

options.text: Commence amoxicillin and clavulanic acid orally for 10 days as antibiotic prophylaxis from time of confirmed rupture
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is avoided in PPROM (associated with necrotising enterocolitis in ORACLE-I); erythromycin is the AU first-line agent. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM)

explanation.summary: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes (PPROM) from 24 to 33+6 weeks is managed expectantly to maximise gestation while monitoring for chorioamnionitis. The AU bundle pairs antenatal corticosteroids, erythromycin (NOT amoxicillin-clavulanate; ORACLE-I showed increased neonatal necrotising enterocolitis), magnesium sulfate neuroprotection within 24 hours of anticipated birth, and inpatient surveillance. Planned delivery is at 36 to 37 weeks. Earlier delivery is indicated for chorioamnionitis, non-reassuring foetal status, or abruption.
key_points: Erythromycin for 10 days; never amoxicillin-clavulanate (NEC risk per ORACLE-I)
Antenatal corticosteroids 24 to 34+6 weeks
Magnesium sulfate neuroprotection if delivery within 24 hours
Plan delivery at 36 to 37 weeks unless infection or compromise
explanation.pearls: Erythromycin 250 mg orally four times daily for 10 days is the AU PPROM prophylactic regimen; amoxicillin-clavulanate is contraindicated by ORACLE-I neonatal necrotising enterocolitis signal.

stem:
A 32-year-old woman attends your reproductive medicine clinic after three first-trimester miscarriages over 18 months, all confirmed by ultrasound as missed miscarriages between 7 and 9 weeks gestation. She had a calf deep vein thrombosis 4 years ago after a long-haul flight, treated with 3 months of warfarin. She is currently not pregnant, has regular menstrual cycles, normal pelvic ultrasound and uterine cavity assessment, and a normal partner semen analysis.

Initial screening 6 weeks ago detected a weakly positive lupus anticoagulant by dilute Russell viper venom time on a single occasion. Anti-cardiolipin IgG and anti-beta2-glycoprotein-I antibodies were both negative. Antinuclear antibody screen was negative. Inherited thrombophilia panel was negative. Karyotype on both partners and products of conception are normal. She asks 'should I just start aspirin and heparin if I get pregnant again?'.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 70, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no oedema, no rash, no synovitis
data_table.Investigations: Lupus anticoagulant weakly positive (dilute Russell viper venom time) on single sample 6 weeks ago; anti-cardiolipin IgG negative; anti-beta2-glycoprotein-I negative; ANA negative; inherited thrombophilia screen negative; parental karyotype normal; products of conception karyotype normal; pelvic ultrasound normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence low-dose aspirin and low-molecular-weight heparin now and continue when she next becomes pregnant
options.rationale: Aspirin and heparin combination treats confirmed antiphospholipid syndrome with obstetric criteria; a single weakly positive lupus anticoagulant is not yet diagnostic and the treatment carries bleeding risk without proven benefit until criteria are met. (Source: ISTH; RANZCOG)

options.text: Refer for in vitro fertilisation with preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy on every embryo created
options.rationale: Recurrent miscarriage without parental karyotypic abnormality or persistent embryonic aneuploidy does not benefit from PGT-A; this leaps over the diagnostic step. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Commence empirical prednisolone 10 mg daily from a positive pregnancy test for empirical immune modulation
options.rationale: Empirical prednisolone for unexplained recurrent miscarriage is not supported by evidence and increases gestational diabetes and preterm birth risk; specific diagnosis comes first. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Refer for hysteroscopy and adhesion assessment under general anaesthetic prior to any further conception attempt
options.rationale: Uterine cavity has already been assessed as normal on imaging; further hysteroscopy without indication is not warranted. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Repeat the lupus anticoagulant assay 12 weeks after the initial positive sample before any treatment decision
options.rationale: Antiphospholipid syndrome (Sapporo / Sydney criteria) requires PERSISTENT positivity of lupus anticoagulant or anticardiolipin or anti-beta2GP-I, confirmed at least 12 weeks apart, plus an obstetric or vascular criterion. A single positive result can reflect transient post-infective antibodies. Aspirin and heparin commit a woman to anticoagulation across pregnancy and should not be started on a single transient result. (Source: ISTH; RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: Antiphospholipid syndrome is diagnosed by persistent (over 12 weeks apart) positivity of lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin, or anti-beta2-glycoprotein-I antibodies, plus obstetric criteria (three or more first-trimester losses, one or more second-trimester loss, or early severe pre-eclampsia or placental insufficiency) or vascular criteria (arterial or venous thromboembolism). A single positive result can be transiently provoked by infection. Treatment of obstetric APS with low-dose aspirin and prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin requires confirmed diagnosis; a transient positive does not justify the bleeding and procedural cost.
key_points: APS diagnosis: persistent positivity 12 weeks apart PLUS clinical criterion
Treat obstetric APS with aspirin + LMWH; never empirically
Single positive lupus anticoagulant: repeat in 12 weeks before labelling
Inherited thrombophilia testing not indicated for unexplained recurrent miscarriage
explanation.pearls: The Sapporo (Sydney) criteria require two persistent positive results at least 12 weeks apart - single positive lupus anticoagulant is not enough to label antiphospholipid syndrome or to start anticoagulation.

stem:
A 31-year-old woman presents to your general practice clinic 4 weeks after the birth of her first child. She tells you 'I cry a few times a day, and I haven't slept more than a few hours straight because the baby feeds every 2 hours'. She describes feeling overwhelmed and irritable, but is bonded with the baby, smiles when interacting, and finds joy in walks with him. She has thoughts that 'it would be easier if I disappeared' but denies active suicidal ideation, plans, or thoughts of harming the baby. There is no past psychiatric history, no substance use, and her partner is supportive.

On examination she is well, well-groomed, with appropriate eye contact and reactive mood. Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale score today is 12 with a score of 1 on the self-harm item. Thyroid function, FBC, and ferritin are within normal limits. Maternal child health nurse review three days ago noted similar sleep deprivation but did not flag concerns.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Well groomed, reactive mood, appropriate eye contact, no psychomotor retardation, no thought disorder
data_table.Investigations: EPDS 12 (item 10 self-harm score 1); FBC, ferritin, TSH within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Provide psychoeducation, social and sleep support, and arrange structured general practice review with repeat EPDS in 2 weeks
options.rationale: An EPDS of 12 with retained interest, intact bond, sleep-deprivation context, and no active suicidal ideation does not yet meet the threshold for major depressive disorder. The COPE perinatal pathway advises psychoeducation, social and sleep support, and a structured 2-week review with repeat EPDS, escalating to psychological therapy if symptoms persist or worsen. Item 10 score of 1 is monitored but does not in itself escalate. (Source: COPE Perinatal Guidelines; RANZCP)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg orally daily and arrange routine general practice review in 4 weeks for breastfeeding monitoring
options.rationale: Sertraline is the AU first-line breastfeeding-compatible SSRI when depression is diagnosed; an EPDS of 12 with intact functioning and bonding does not yet meet that threshold. (Source: COPE Perinatal Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: Admit to a mother-and-baby unit for inpatient assessment and care for safety concerns this evening
options.rationale: Inpatient admission is reserved for severe perinatal depression with risk to self or baby, psychosis, or significant functional impairment; her presentation does not meet criteria. (Source: COPE Perinatal Guidelines)

options.text: Refer urgently to perinatal psychiatry within 24 hours for diagnostic interview and pharmacological plan formulation
options.rationale: Urgent psychiatry referral is reserved for higher EPDS scores with active suicidal ideation, psychosis, or risk to infant; structured general practice review with safety net is appropriate first. (Source: COPE Perinatal Guidelines)

options.text: Suggest that she stops breastfeeding and switches to formula to allow her partner to take over feeds at night
options.rationale: Stopping breastfeeding is not part of first-line management and can worsen distress and bonding; partner-supported expressed-milk feeding overnight can help without stopping. (Source: COPE Perinatal Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Postnatal blues occur in around 80% of women between days 3 and 14, are self-limited, and do not require pharmacotherapy. Postnatal depression is differentiated by persistent low mood, loss of interest, impaired functioning, or thoughts of harm beyond 2 weeks. EPDS over 13 raises concern; item 10 (self-harm) of 1 or more is monitored irrespective of total. AU first-line for diagnosed postnatal depression is breastfeeding-compatible SSRI (sertraline first, paroxetine acceptable) with psychological therapy; mother-and-baby unit admission is reserved for severity, risk, or psychosis.
key_points: Blues: days 3-14, self-limited, sleep + support
Depression: EPDS over 13 or persistent impairment past 2 weeks
Item 10 of 1+ on EPDS: explicit safety review even at lower total scores
Sertraline first-line breastfeeding-compatible SSRI; paroxetine acceptable
explanation.pearls: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily is the AU first-line breastfeeding-compatible SSRI when major postnatal depression is established; relative infant dose is well below the 10% safety threshold.

stem:
A 46-year-old man attends your general practice clinic with his partner asking for help to stop drinking. He has been drinking around 12 standard drinks of beer daily for the last 3 years. His most recent drink was 4 hours ago. He says 'my hands get a bit shaky in the mornings until I have my first drink'. He has had no alcohol withdrawal seizures, no delirium tremens, no recent admissions, and no other major medical comorbidity. He is not currently suicidal. His partner is supportive and they live together.

On examination he is anxious with a mild postural tremor. His Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol - revised (CIWA-Ar) score is 9 with no autonomic instability. Pulse, blood pressure, and temperature are within normal limits. Bloods show transaminases mildly elevated (AST 60, ALT 50), MCV 102 fL (80-100), and platelet count 142 x10^9/L (150-400). He has a stable home, a willing supporter, no benzodiazepine use, no opioid use, and access to daily review.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92, BP 138/82 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Anxious, mild postural tremor, no nystagmus, no ataxia, no confusion
data_table.Investigations: CIWA-Ar 9; AST 60 U/L, ALT 50 U/L, MCV 102 fL (80-100), platelets 142 x10^9/L (150-400); UEC and glucose within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit to the inpatient acute medical unit for medically supervised withdrawal and intravenous benzodiazepine infusion
options.rationale: Inpatient management is reserved for severe withdrawal, prior withdrawal seizures or delirium tremens, lack of supports, severe medical or psychiatric comorbidity, or polysubstance withdrawal; this patient is at low risk and home-based management is appropriate. (Source: RACP Alcohol Treatment Guidelines)

options.text: Home-based withdrawal with diazepam loading regimen, oral thiamine, daily clinical review, and safety supervision
options.rationale: Low-risk patients (mild withdrawal, no prior seizure or delirium, intact supports, no severe comorbidity) are AU candidates for outpatient withdrawal with diazepam loading (e.g. 20 mg every 2 hours symptom-triggered or fixed-dose regimen) plus oral thiamine. Daily clinical review, dispense control, and a supervising partner reduce diversion and detect escalation. Higher CIWA-Ar (over 15), seizure history, delirium, or unsupported living shifts to inpatient. (Source: RACP Alcohol Treatment Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction)

options.text: Commence oral disulfiram 200 mg daily immediately without any thiamine cover for prevention of relapse
options.rationale: Disulfiram is a relapse-prevention adjunct after withdrawal completion, requires informed consent and supervised dispensing, and risks severe disulfiram-ethanol reaction; it is not started before withdrawal. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction)

options.text: Prescribe oral acamprosate 666 mg three times daily and advise sudden cessation of alcohol from tonight
options.rationale: Acamprosate is a relapse-prevention agent after withdrawal completion and does not treat acute withdrawal; sudden cessation without benzodiazepine cover risks seizure and delirium tremens. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Reassure that 12 standard drinks daily is not high enough to need withdrawal management and advise abrupt cessation
options.rationale: Twelve standard drinks daily over years is dependence; abrupt cessation without medical support risks seizure, delirium tremens, and Wernicke. (Source: RACP Alcohol Treatment Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Alcohol withdrawal severity is risk-stratified using CIWA-Ar, drinking pattern, prior withdrawal complications, comorbidity, and supports. Home-based withdrawal with diazepam loading and oral thiamine is safe for low-risk patients (CIWA-Ar under 15, no prior seizure or delirium, no major comorbidity, supportive home). Inpatient management is required for high-risk presentations. Thiamine 300 mg orally daily covers most patients; intravenous thiamine 100 mg pre-glucose is essential if Wernicke is suspected. Relapse-prevention agents (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram) are layered after withdrawal completion.
key_points: Outpatient withdrawal safe for low-risk patients with supports
Diazepam loading symptom-triggered or fixed schedule; thiamine cover always
Inpatient: prior seizure or DT, severe withdrawal, unsupported, comorbidity
Relapse prevention starts after withdrawal completion, not during
explanation.pearls: Oral thiamine 300 mg daily for 5 days, then 100 mg daily, accompanies every alcohol withdrawal regardless of setting; intravenous thiamine 100 mg precedes any dextrose-containing fluid if Wernicke is suspected.

stem:
A 28-year-old graphic designer attends your general practice clinic with 18 months of persistent worry about her job security, finances, and relationships. She says 'the worry is always running in the background and I can't switch it off, even when nothing is wrong'. She has poor sleep onset, muscle tension, fatigue, and difficulty concentrating. She avoids social events. She drinks 2-3 standard drinks weekly. She has no past psychiatric history, no substance use disorder, no suicidal ideation, and no medical comorbidity.

On mental state examination she is anxious-appearing with reactive mood and intact reality testing. GAD-7 score is 16. PHQ-9 score is 9 (no anhedonia, no suicidal ideation). Thyroid function, electrolytes, and ECG are within normal limits. She is keen to engage with treatment but has not previously tried psychological therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 84, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Anxious affect, reactive mood, no thought disorder, intact reality testing, no psychomotor abnormality
data_table.Investigations: GAD-7 16, PHQ-9 9; TSH 1.6 mIU/L; UEC and ECG within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg three times daily for 6 weeks with weekly general practice clinic reviews for tolerance
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not first-line in generalised anxiety disorder because of dependence risk and effect on cognitive performance; reserve for short-term distress crises. (Source: RANZCP CPG Anxiety Disorders; AMH)

options.text: Propranolol 20 mg three times daily as ongoing monotherapy for the persistent worry and tension symptoms
options.rationale: Propranolol addresses peripheral autonomic features of performance anxiety; it is not effective for the cognitive worry of generalised anxiety disorder. (Source: RANZCP CPG Anxiety Disorders)

options.text: Escitalopram 10 mg orally daily commenced alongside cognitive behavioural therapy referral and lifestyle work
options.rationale: Generalised anxiety disorder with significant functional impairment (GAD-7 over 15) is treated with a combination of cognitive behavioural therapy and an SSRI or SNRI; escitalopram and sertraline are AU first-line SSRI options. CBT alone is reasonable for milder severity but combined treatment improves outcomes at this severity. Avoid benzodiazepines and pregabalin first-line. (Source: RANZCP CPG Anxiety Disorders; AMH)

options.text: Pregabalin 75 mg twice daily titrated to 300 mg total daily dose over the next 2 weeks as the first option
options.rationale: Pregabalin is effective for generalised anxiety disorder but is third-line in AU practice due to dependence and divertibility; SSRI plus CBT is first-line. (Source: AMH; RANZCP CPG Anxiety Disorders)

options.text: Mirtazapine 15 mg orally at bedtime as the first-line option given the sleep onset disturbance she reports today
options.rationale: Mirtazapine is not first-line for generalised anxiety disorder; it is reserved for depression with insomnia. Targeting sleep symptoms first does not address the underlying anxiety disorder. (Source: RANZCP CPG Anxiety Disorders)

explanation.summary: Generalised anxiety disorder is treated stepwise. Milder cases respond to psychoeducation, lifestyle, and cognitive behavioural therapy. Moderate to severe presentations (GAD-7 over 15 with functional impairment) combine an SSRI (escitalopram, sertraline) or SNRI (venlafaxine) with CBT. Benzodiazepines and pregabalin are reserved for refractory or short-term symptom relief because of dependence risk. Antidepressants take 4 to 6 weeks for full effect, with transient anxiety worsening possible in the first 2 weeks of SSRI treatment.
key_points: First-line: SSRI plus CBT for moderate-severe GAD
CBT alone reasonable for milder presentations
Benzodiazepines, pregabalin not first-line - dependence risk
Counsel about initial anxiety worsening in first 2 weeks of SSRI
explanation.pearls: Escitalopram 10 mg orally daily is the AU first-line SSRI for generalised anxiety disorder; counsel that anxiety may transiently worsen in the first two weeks before steady-state benefit at four to six weeks.

subtopic_detail: Metabolic syndrome on olanzapine and switch decision
stem:
A 27-year-old man attends your community mental health clinic for 6-month review after his first episode of psychosis. He has been on olanzapine 15 mg orally daily and is symptom-free, working full-time, and adherent. He says 'I feel well on this one, the others gave me side effects'. He has gained 8 kg since starting, and his trousers no longer fit. He has no family history of cardiovascular disease but his maternal grandfather had type 2 diabetes.

On examination his weight is 88 kg (was 80 kg), waist circumference 102 cm, blood pressure 138/86 mmHg, and pulse 80 regular. Fasting glucose today is 7.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.5), HbA1c 6.0% (under 6.5), fasting triglycerides 3.2 mmol/L (under 1.7), HDL 0.9 mmol/L (over 1.0), and LDL 3.0 mmol/L (under 3.5). Liver function tests and renal function are within normal limits. ECG QTc is 420 ms.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 80, BP 138/86 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Weight 88 kg, waist circumference 102 cm, BMI 28; no acanthosis nigricans, no oedema
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 7.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.5), HbA1c 6.0% (under 6.5), triglycerides 3.2 mmol/L (under 1.7), HDL 0.9 mmol/L (over 1.0), LDL 3.0 mmol/L (under 3.5); LFTs and UEC within normal limits; ECG QTc 420 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Continue olanzapine unchanged and review weight, lipid, and glucose parameters again in 12 months without intervening
options.rationale: Findings already meet metabolic syndrome criteria with impaired fasting glucose; deferring intervention 12 months risks progression to type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular event. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia; eTG Endocrinology)

options.text: Initiate lifestyle programme, repeat fasting glucose to confirm diabetes, and switch to aripiprazole with cross-titration
options.rationale: Olanzapine has the highest metabolic burden of the second-generation antipsychotics; in first-episode psychosis with emerging metabolic syndrome and impaired fasting glucose, AU practice is to confirm the glucose result (repeat or HbA1c trend), initiate intensive lifestyle programme, and switch to a lower metabolic burden agent (aripiprazole, lurasidone, or amisulpride) via cross-titration. The choice balances metabolic safety against relapse risk. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia; AMH)

options.text: Add metformin 500 mg twice daily to olanzapine without changing antipsychotic, weight loss, or lifestyle programme
options.rationale: Metformin can mitigate antipsychotic-associated weight gain but is adjunctive; switching the offending agent and lifestyle programme remain core. (Source: AMH; eTG Endocrinology)

options.text: Stop olanzapine immediately and commence high-dose haloperidol depot intramuscularly with regular community review
options.rationale: Abrupt switching risks relapse and movement disorders; haloperidol carries extrapyramidal burden inappropriate for first-episode psychosis remission. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Refer to bariatric surgery clinic for sleeve gastrectomy assessment given the persisting weight gain on olanzapine therapy
options.rationale: Bariatric surgery referral is premature; switch antipsychotic and trial intensive lifestyle and pharmacotherapy first. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic-associated metabolic syndrome is a major cause of premature cardiovascular mortality in psychosis. Olanzapine and clozapine carry the highest metabolic burden; aripiprazole, lurasidone, ziprasidone, and amisulpride carry low to moderate burden. AU monitoring (weight, waist, BP, lipids, glucose) is at baseline, weekly initially, at 3 months, then yearly. Emerging metabolic syndrome warrants intensive lifestyle programme, switch to lower-burden agent with cross-titration, and consideration of metformin adjunct. Clozapine usually stays despite metabolic cost given relapse risk.
key_points: Olanzapine: highest metabolic burden after clozapine
Switch to aripiprazole or lurasidone via cross-titration for emerging metabolic syndrome
Lifestyle programme + metformin adjunct for weight if switch insufficient
Monitor weight, waist, BP, lipids, glucose at baseline, 3 months, then yearly
explanation.pearls: Aripiprazole 10-15 mg orally daily is the AU preferred switch target from olanzapine for emerging metabolic syndrome in first-episode psychosis given its low metabolic burden and partial dopamine agonism.

stem:
A 19-year-old apprentice presents to your community mental health intake with a 4-week history of persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations (running commentary, two voices). He had been smoking high-potency cannabis daily for the past 2 years; he stopped 4 weeks ago after the symptoms started. He is brought by his older sister who says 'he won't come out of his room and he thinks the neighbours are tapping the walls'. He denies suicidal ideation but is socially withdrawn and is not sleeping.

On mental state examination he is suspicious, with thought disorder evident as loosening of associations and tangential speech. There is third-person auditory hallucination and persecutory delusion. Insight is impaired. Urine drug screen 3 weeks ago and again today is negative for cannabis metabolites. Blood tests including TSH, vitamin B12, and HIV serology are pending. CT brain (taken at presentation) is normal. He lives with his sister and parents who are supportive.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 124/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Thought disorder, persecutory delusion, third-person auditory hallucination, impaired insight, no movement disorder
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen negative at week 3 and week 4; FBC, electrolytes within normal limits; TSH, vitamin B12, HIV serology pending; non-contrast CT brain normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Reassure that symptoms will resolve once cannabis fully clears the system and arrange community follow-up in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Persisting psychosis 4 weeks after cessation has crossed the substance-induced threshold and requires antipsychotic therapy and structured early intervention; symptoms may not self-resolve. (Source: Orygen; EPPIC; RANZCP)

options.text: Initiate haloperidol 5 mg intramuscularly stat now and admit involuntarily under the state mental health act
options.rationale: Involuntary admission and IM high-potency typical antipsychotic is reserved for risk-driven presentations not amenable to voluntary care; this patient has family support and is engageable. (Source: RANZCP)

options.text: Refer to the gambling and addiction service to address cannabis dependence before any psychotic symptom treatment
options.rationale: Cannabis cessation is essential but does not replace antipsychotic therapy when psychotic symptoms persist; both run in parallel. (Source: Orygen)

options.text: Prescribe clozapine 12.5 mg orally and arrange weekly haematological monitoring as the next pharmacological step
options.rationale: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (failure of two adequate antipsychotic trials at adequate dose and duration); not first-line in early psychosis. (Source: RANZCP)

options.text: Refer to early psychosis service for assertive care, commence low-dose risperidone, and continue cannabis-cessation work
options.rationale: Persistent psychotic symptoms 4 weeks after cannabis cessation meet criteria for first-episode psychosis. The AU early-intervention model (EPPIC/Orygen) pairs low-dose second-generation antipsychotic (risperidone, aripiprazole, or amisulpride at low dose), structured assertive case management, family education, and ongoing cannabis-cessation support. Early intervention reduces relapse and improves functional outcome. (Source: Orygen; EPPIC; RANZCP)

explanation.summary: First-episode psychosis in young people benefits from assertive multidisciplinary care via early intervention services. Low-dose second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, aripiprazole, amisulpride, olanzapine) are AU first-line, paired with family work, vocational support, cannabis-cessation work, and psychological therapy. Symptoms persisting beyond 4 weeks after substance cessation argue against a transient substance-induced diagnosis. Clozapine is reserved for two-trial-failed treatment resistance. Metabolic monitoring is critical (see related questions in this batch).
key_points: Persisting psychosis 4+ weeks after cessation moves diagnosis past substance-induced
First-line: low-dose risperidone, aripiprazole, or amisulpride
Assertive case management and family work improve relapse and functional outcomes
Clozapine reserved for two adequate trials failed - not first-line
explanation.pearls: Risperidone 1-2 mg orally daily is the AU starting dose in first-episode psychosis; this is well below the schizophrenia maintenance dose and minimises extrapyramidal adverse effects.
